revision.comment: 飛鳥時代の人物。壬申の乱のとは倭京の留守司で、大海人皇子に内応した
revision.text:
'''坂上熊毛'''（さかのうえのくまけ、生没年不明）は、日本の[[飛鳥時代]]の人物である。旧仮名遣いでの読みは「さかのうへのくまけ」。姓（[[カバネ]]）は[[直]]。[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]のとき倭京の[[留守司]]で、大海人皇子側に内応した。
[[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。
壬申の乱が起こったとき、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。熊毛について他に知られることはない。
[[Category:坂上氏|さかのうえのくまけ]] [[Category:壬申の乱||さかのうえのくまけ]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の人物|さかのうえのくまけ]]

壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。熊毛について他に知られることはない。

revision.comment: 父と祖父について
revision.text:
'''坂上熊毛'''（さかのうえのくまけ、生没年不明）は、日本の[[飛鳥時代]]の人物である。旧仮名遣いでの読みは「さかのうへのくまけ」。姓（[[カバネ]]）は[[直]]。[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]のとき[[飛鳥|倭京]]の[[留守司]]で、大海人皇子側に内応した。
「坂上系図」によれば、父は[[坂上甲由|甲由]]、祖父は[[坂上駒子|駒子]]。同じく壬申の乱で活躍した[[坂上老]]は従兄弟である。父が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。 [[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。
壬申の乱が起こったとき、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。熊毛について他に知られることはない。
== 参考文献 == *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。
[[Category:坂上氏|さかのうえのくまけ]] [[Category:壬申の乱|さかのうえのくまけ]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の人物|さかのうえのくまけ]]

revision.comment: 出自の異説
revision.text:
'''坂上 熊毛'''（さかのうえ の くまけ<ref>旧仮名遣いでの読みは「さかのうへのくまけ」</ref>、生没年不詳）は、日本の[[飛鳥時代]]の人物である。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直]]。[[冠位・位階制度の変遷#冠位二十六階|冠位]]は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]<ref name="a">「坂上系図」（『続群書類従』巻第185所収）</ref>。
[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]のとき[[飛鳥|倭京]]の[[留守司]]であったが、[[大海人皇子]]側に内応した。
== 出自 == 「坂上系図」<ref name="a" />によれば、熊毛を東漢駒子<ref>いつから坂上直姓を称したのか明らかではない。欽明・敏達朝の子麻呂が東漢坂上の複姓を持っていたことから、坂上直姓を称したのは敏達朝以降と考えられる。</ref>の孫で、坂上甲由の子とする。また、駒子と甲由の間に子麻呂を置き、甲由を申田の誤りとして、熊毛を東漢坂上子麻呂の孫で、坂上申田の子とする系図もある<ref>鈴木真年『百家系図稿』巻9,東漢直（宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 による）</ref>。
同じく壬申の乱で活躍した[[坂上老]]は従兄弟である。父の甲由が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。 [[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。
== 経歴 == 壬申の乱が起こったとき、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。熊毛について他に知られることはない。
== 参考文献 == *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかのうえ の くまけ}} [[Category:坂上氏|くまけ]] [[Category:壬申の乱]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の人物]]

revision.comment: 子息宗大に対する賜田
revision.text:
'''坂上 熊毛'''（さかのうえ の くまけ<ref>旧仮名遣いでの読みは「さかのうへのくまけ」</ref>、生没年不詳）は、日本の[[飛鳥時代]]の人物である。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直]]。[[冠位・位階制度の変遷#冠位二十六階|冠位]]は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]<ref name="a">「坂上系図」（『続群書類従』巻第185所収）</ref>。
[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]のとき[[飛鳥|倭京]]の[[留守司]]であったが、[[大海人皇子]]側に内応した。
== 出自 == 「坂上系図」<ref name="a" />によれば、熊毛を東漢駒子<ref>いつから坂上直姓を称したのか明らかではない。欽明・敏達朝の子麻呂が東漢坂上の複姓を持っていたことから、坂上直姓を称したのは敏達朝以降と考えられる。</ref>の孫で、坂上甲由の子とする。また、駒子と甲由の間に子麻呂を置き、甲由を申田の誤りとして、熊毛を東漢坂上子麻呂の孫で、坂上申田の子とする系図もある<ref>鈴木真年『百家系図稿』巻9,東漢直（宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 による）</ref>。
父の甲由が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。 [[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。
== 経歴 == 壬申の乱が起こったとき、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。
[[霊亀]]2年（[[716年]]）4月8日壬申の乱における熊毛の功に対し、その子である宗大は田を賜与された。
== 参考文献 == *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}

[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]のとき[[飛鳥|倭京]]の[[留守司]]であったが、大海人皇子（[[天武天皇]]）側に内応した。
== 出自 == 「坂上系図」<ref name="a" />によれば、熊毛を東漢駒子<ref>いつから坂上直姓を称したのか明らかではない。欽明・敏達朝の子麻呂が東漢坂上の複姓を持っていたことから、坂上直姓を称したのは敏達朝以降と考えられる。</ref>の孫で、坂上甲由の子とする。また、駒子と甲由の間に子麻呂を置き、甲由を申田の誤りとして、熊毛を東漢坂上子麻呂の孫で、坂上申田の子とする系図もある<ref>鈴木真年『百家系図稿』巻9,東漢直（宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 による）</ref>。
父の甲由が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。 [[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。
== 経歴 == 壬申の乱が起こったとき、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。
[[霊亀]]2年（[[716年]]）4月8日壬申の乱における熊毛の功に対し、その子である宗大は田を賜与された。
== 参考文献 == *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。

revision.comment: 文献注
revision.text:
'''坂上 熊毛'''（さかのうえ の くまけ<ref>旧仮名遣いでの読みは「さかのうへのくまけ」</ref>、生没年不詳）は、日本の[[飛鳥時代]]の人物である。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直]]。[[冠位・位階制度の変遷#冠位二十六階|冠位]]は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]。[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]のとき[[飛鳥|倭京]]の[[留守司]]であったが、大海人皇子（[[天武天皇]]）側に内応した。
== 出自 == [[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。子に[[坂上宗大]]がいる。
「坂上系図」<ref>「坂上系図」（『続群書類従』巻第185所収）</ref>によれば、熊毛を東漢駒子<ref>いつから坂上直姓を称したのか明らかではない。欽明・敏達朝の子麻呂が東漢坂上の複姓を持っていたことから、坂上直姓を称したのは敏達朝以降と考えられる。</ref>の孫で、坂上甲由の子とする。また、駒子と甲由の間に子麻呂を置き、甲由を申田の誤りとして、熊毛を東漢坂上子麻呂の孫で、坂上申田の子とする系図もある<ref>鈴木真年『百家系図稿』巻9,東漢直（宝賀寿男『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 による）</ref>。父の甲由が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。
== 経歴 == 壬申の乱が起こったとき、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった<ref>次の段落とともに、『日本書紀』巻28、天武天皇元年6月己丑（29日）条に続く是日条、新編日本古典文学全集第3分冊322-325頁にもとづく。</ref>。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。
[[霊亀]]2年（[[716年]]）4月8日、壬申の年の功に対し、熊毛の子である[[坂上宗大]]が田を賜与された<ref>『続日本紀』巻7、霊亀3年4月癸丑（8日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第2分冊8-9頁。</ref>。天平宝字元年（[[757年]]）12月9日に、太政官は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]坂上直熊毛の功を論じて中功とし、[[功田]]6町を2世にわたって伝えると決定した<ref>『続日本紀』巻20、天平宝字元年12月壬子（9日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第3分冊240-241頁。</ref>。熊毛は生前に6町の功田を授かり、これが宗大に伝えられ、中功と確定したのであろう。
== 参考文献 == *[[小島憲之]]・[[直木孝次郎]]・[[西宮一民]]・[[蔵中進]]・[[毛利正守]]校訂・訳『[[日本書紀]]』3、小学館（新編日本古典文学全集 4）、1998年。 *[[青木和夫]]・[[稲岡耕二]]・[[笹山晴生]]・[[白藤禮幸]]校注『[[続日本紀]]』二（新日本古典文学大系13）、岩波書店、1990年。 *青木和夫・稲岡耕二・笹山晴生・白藤禮幸校注『続日本紀』三（新日本古典文学大系14）、岩波書店、1991年。 *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}

「坂上系図」<ref>「坂上系図」（『続群書類従』巻第185所収）</ref>によれば、熊毛を[[坂上駒子|東漢駒子]]<ref>いつから坂上直姓を称したのか明らかではない。[[欽明天皇|欽明]]・[[敏達天皇|敏達]]朝の[[坂上子麻呂]]が東漢坂上の複姓を持っていたことから、坂上直姓を称したのは敏達朝以降と考えられる。</ref>の孫で、[[坂上甲由]]の子とする。また、駒子と甲由の間に子麻呂を置き、甲由を申田の誤りとして、熊毛を東漢坂上子麻呂の孫で、坂上申田の子とする系図もある<ref>[[鈴木真年]]『百家系図稿』巻9,東漢直（[[宝賀寿男]]『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 による）</ref>。父の甲由が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。
== 経歴 == 壬申の乱が起こったとき、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった<ref>次の段落とともに、『日本書紀』巻28、天武天皇元年6月己丑（29日）条に続く是日条、新編日本古典文学全集第3分冊322-325頁にもとづく。</ref>。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]とともに軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵をめざして人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。
[[霊亀]]2年（[[716年]]）4月8日、壬申の年の功に対し、熊毛の子である[[坂上宗大]]が田を賜与された<ref>『続日本紀』巻7、霊亀3年4月癸丑（8日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第2分冊8-9頁。</ref>。天平宝字元年（[[757年]]）12月9日に、太政官は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]坂上直熊毛の功を論じて中功とし、[[功田]]6町を2世にわたって伝えると決定した<ref>『続日本紀』巻20、天平宝字元年12月壬子（9日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第3分冊240-241頁。</ref>。熊毛は生前に6町の功田を授かり、これが宗大に伝えられ、中功と確定したのであろう。
== 参考文献 == *[[小島憲之]]・[[直木孝次郎]]・[[西宮一民]]・[[蔵中進]]・[[毛利正守]]校訂・訳『[[日本書紀]]』3、小学館（新編日本古典文学全集 4）、1998年。 *[[青木和夫]]・[[稲岡耕二]]・[[笹山晴生]]・[[白藤禮幸]]校注『[[続日本紀]]』二（新日本古典文学大系13）、岩波書店、1990年。 *青木和夫・稲岡耕二・笹山晴生・白藤禮幸校注『続日本紀』三（新日本古典文学大系14）、岩波書店、1991年。 *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 坂上熊毛 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[飛鳥時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = [[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]] | 主君 = [[弘文天皇|大友皇子]]→[[天武天皇|大海人皇子]] | 氏族 = [[坂上氏|坂上]][[直 (姓)|直]] | 父母 = 父：坂上甲由 | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[坂上宗大|宗大]] | 特記事項 = }}
'''坂上 熊毛'''（さかのうえ の くまけ<ref>旧仮名遣いでの読みは「さかのうへのくまけ」</ref>）は、[[飛鳥時代]]の人物。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]。[[冠位二十六階|冠位]]は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]。[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]の際[[飛鳥|倭京]]の[[留守司]]であったが、大海人皇子（[[天武天皇]]）側に内応した。
== 出自 == [[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。子に[[坂上宗大|宗大]]がいる。
「坂上系図」<ref>「坂上系図」（『続群書類従』巻第185所収）</ref>によれば、熊毛を[[坂上駒子|東漢駒子]]<ref>いつから坂上直姓を称したのか明らかではない。[[欽明天皇|欽明]]・[[敏達天皇|敏達]]朝の[[坂上子麻呂]]が東漢坂上の複姓を持っていたことから、坂上直姓を称したのは敏達朝以降と考えられる。</ref>の孫で、[[坂上甲由]]の子とする。また、駒子と甲由の間に子麻呂を置き、甲由を申田の誤りとして、熊毛を東漢坂上子麻呂の孫で、坂上申田の子とする系図もある<ref>[[鈴木真年]]『百家系図稿』巻9,東漢直（[[宝賀寿男]]『古代氏族系譜集成』古代氏族研究会、1986年 による）</ref>。父の甲由が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。
== 経歴 == 壬申の乱が起こった際、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった<ref>次の段落と共に、『日本書紀』巻28、天武天皇元年6月己丑（29日）条に続く是日条、新編日本古典文学全集第3分冊322-325頁にもとづく。</ref>。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江大津宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]と共に軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵を目指して人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。
[[霊亀]]2年（[[716年]]）4月8日、壬申の年の功に対し、熊毛の子である[[坂上宗大]]が田を賜与された<ref>『続日本紀』巻7、霊亀3年4月癸丑（8日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第2分冊8-9頁。</ref>。天平宝字元年（[[757年]]）12月9日に、太政官は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]坂上直熊毛の功を論じて中功とし、[[功田]]6町を2世にわたって伝えると決定した<ref>『続日本紀』巻20、天平宝字元年12月壬子（9日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第3分冊240-241頁。</ref>。熊毛は生前に6町の功田を授かり、これが宗大に伝えられ、中功と確定したのであろう。
== 参考文献 == *[[小島憲之]]・[[直木孝次郎]]・[[西宮一民]]・[[蔵中進]]・[[毛利正守]]校訂・訳『[[日本書紀]]』3、小学館（新編日本古典文学全集 4）、1998年。 *[[青木和夫]]・[[稲岡耕二]]・[[笹山晴生]]・[[白藤禮幸]]校注『[[続日本紀]]』二（新日本古典文学大系13）、岩波書店、1990年。 *青木和夫・稲岡耕二・笹山晴生・白藤禮幸校注『続日本紀』三（新日本古典文学大系14）、岩波書店、1991年。 *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。

revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References ==
* {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." revision.comment: deciding to rename this category to a more descriptive one
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.

revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. revision.comment: Text replacement - "-qq]]" to "-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]]"
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qqqqqqqqqq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>

Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
