revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:completion tier 3]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pgoodbye $1]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "大和国" to "Yamato Province "
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qq" to "11"
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|12=draft}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=draft}}. He held the rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|12=draft}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=draft}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=draft}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏|12=draft}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=draft}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=draft}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=draft}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=draft}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|12=draft}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=draft}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡|12=draft}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮|12=draft}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=draft}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=draft}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=draft}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=draft}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|12=draft}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|12=draft}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=draft}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|12=draft}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|12=draft}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=draft}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=draft}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=draft}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=draft}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=draft|12=draft}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. revision.comment: Text replacement - "rank" to "Rank"
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft|12=draft}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|12=draft|12=draft}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=draft|12=draft}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|12=draft|12=draft}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=draft|12=draft}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=draft|12=draft}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=draft|12=draft}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=draft|12=draft}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=draft|12=draft}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft|12=draft}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=draft|12=draft}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=draft|12=draft}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=draft|12=draft}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=draft|12=draft}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|12=draft|12=draft}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=draft|12=draft}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|12=draft|12=draft}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|12=draft|12=draft}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=draft|12=draft}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=draft|12=draft}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=draft|12=draft}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=draft|12=draft}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: 14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:坂上熊毛 up to rev 88413779
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 坂上熊毛 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[飛鳥時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = [[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]] | 主君 = [[弘文天皇|大友皇子]]→[[天武天皇|大海人皇子]] | 氏族 = [[坂上氏|坂上]][[直 (姓)|直]] | 父母 = 父：坂上甲由 | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[坂上宗大|宗大]] | 特記事項 = }}
'''坂上 熊毛'''（さかのうえ の くまけ<ref>旧仮名遣いでの読みは「さかのうへのくまけ」</ref>）は、[[飛鳥時代]]の人物。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]。[[冠位二十六階|冠位]]は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]。[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]の際[[飛鳥|倭京]]の[[留守司]]であったが、大海人皇子（[[天武天皇]]）側に内応した。
== 出自 == [[坂上氏]]は渡来系の倭漢氏（[[東漢氏]]）の一族である。東漢氏は支族が多く、[[大和国]]南部に本拠を持ち、軍事に関与することが多かった。子に[[坂上宗大|宗大]]がいる。
「坂上系図」<ref>「坂上系図」（『続群書類従』巻第185所収）</ref>によれば、熊毛を[[坂上駒子|東漢駒子]]<ref>いつから坂上直姓を称したのか明らかではない。[[欽明天皇|欽明]]・[[敏達天皇|敏達]]朝の[[坂上子麻呂]]が東漢坂上の複姓を持っていたことから、坂上直姓を称したのは敏達朝以降と考えられる。</ref>の孫で、[[坂上甲由]]の子とする。父の甲由が[[大和国]][[高市郡]]の出とされるので、熊毛も同じ可能性が高い。
== 経歴 == 壬申の乱が起こった際、坂上熊毛は倭京の留守司であった<ref>次の段落と共に、『日本書紀』巻28、天武天皇元年6月己丑（29日）条に続く是日条、新編日本古典文学全集第3分冊322-325頁にもとづく。</ref>。倭京の倭は「やまと」と読み、当時近江にあった都と別に留守司が管理していた。『[[日本書紀]]』が伝える留守司には他に[[高坂王]]があり、熊毛は王の同僚か部下であったと推測される。
壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月下旬に大海人皇子が兵を興すと、[[近江大津宮]]にいた大友皇子（[[弘文天皇]]）は倭京に使者を派遣して軍を発することを命じた。高坂王は使者の[[穂積百足]]、[[穂積五百枝]]、[[物部日向]]と共に軍の編成を始めた。しかしこのとき、大海人皇子側につくことを決めた[[大伴吹負]]は挙兵を目指して人数を集め、坂上熊毛と相談して、一二の漢直（倭漢直）に内応を求めた。6月29日、まず[[秦熊]]が使者のふりをして馬に乗って馳せ、「[[高市皇子]]が不破から来た。軍衆が多く従っている」と誤報を流した。陣営の兵士は驚いて逃げ散った。それから数十騎で乗り込むと、熊毛と諸々の直が内応したため、吹負は難なく指揮権を奪取した。
[[霊亀]]2年（[[716年]]）4月8日、壬申の年の功に対し、熊毛の子である[[坂上宗大]]が田を賜与された<ref>『続日本紀』巻7、霊亀3年4月癸丑（8日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第2分冊8-9頁。</ref>。天平宝字元年（[[757年]]）12月9日に、太政官は[[贈位|贈]][[大錦下]]坂上直熊毛の功を論じて中功とし、[[功田]]6町を2世にわたって伝えると決定した<ref>『続日本紀』巻20、天平宝字元年12月壬子（9日）条、新日本古典文学大系版第3分冊240-241頁。</ref>。熊毛は生前に6町の功田を授かり、これが宗大に伝えられ、中功と確定したのであろう。
== 参考文献 == *[[小島憲之]]・[[直木孝次郎]]・[[西宮一民]]・[[蔵中進]]・[[毛利正守]]校訂・訳『[[日本書紀]]』3、小学館（新編日本古典文学全集 4）、1998年。 *[[青木和夫]]・[[稲岡耕二]]・[[笹山晴生]]・[[白藤禮幸]]校注『[[続日本紀]]』二（新日本古典文学大系13）、岩波書店、1990年。 *青木和夫・稲岡耕二・笹山晴生・白藤禮幸校注『続日本紀』三（新日本古典文学大系14）、岩波書店、1991年。 *[[直木孝次郎]]「壬申の乱と坂上氏」、『続日本紀研究』、1巻1号、1954年1月。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:さかのうえ の くまけ}} [[Category:坂上氏|くまけ]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の人物]] [[Category:壬申の乱]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
