'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka period|qq=}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|qq=}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinshin War|qq=}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kinmei|qq=}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Bidatsu|qq=}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takichi District, Yamato Province|qq=}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinshin War|qq=}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|qq=}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|qq=}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiki|qq=}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Hōji|qq=}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|qq=}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiki|qq=}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Hōji|qq=}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|qq=}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|天平宝字|" to "|天平宝字|1=Tenpyō-hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|"
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka period|qq=}} in Japan. His {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|qq=}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinshin War|qq=}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|東漢氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国|qq=Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kinmei|qq=}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Bidatsu|qq=}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takichi District, Yamato Province|ja|高市郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takichi District, Yamato Province|qq=}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinshin War|qq=}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|ja|近江大津宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|qq=}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|qq=}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki|ja|霊亀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiki|qq=}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|1=Tenpyō-hōji|2=ja|3=天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Hōji|qq=}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|qq=}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>

Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:壬申の乱
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Bot: update category → Sakanoue clan
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "�]]" to "]]"
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Jinshin War participants
revision.text:
'''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.comment: Bot: append |1= |comment= |WD= in {{ill}}
revision.text:
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|qid=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=|1=Mononobe no Hinata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=|1=功田|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|qid=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
