revision.text:
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|qid=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

<!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--> [[Category:Sakanoue clan]] [[Category:Jinshin War]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11424804}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|qid=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|qq=|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|qq=|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|qq=|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|qq=|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|qq=|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|qq=|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|qq=|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|qq=|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|qq=|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|qq=|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|qq=|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." <!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--> [[Category:Sakanoue clan]] [[Category:Jinshin War]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11424804}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|qq=draft|" to "|"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>

''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

<!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--><!--Sakagami no Kumake--> [[Category:Sakanoue clan]] [[Category:Jinshin War]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11424804}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]" to ""
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.

On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] in Japan. His [[:en:Kabane|kabane]] was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the [[:en:Yamatonoaya clan|Yamato no Aya clan]], a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]], it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takichi District, Yamato Province]], it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]], Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in [[:en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]].<ref>The following details are based on the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In [[:en:Reiki (era)|Reiki]] 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757), the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takechi|en|Prince Takechi|WD=Q837590|lt=Prince Takeda}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki (era)|en|Reiki (era)|WD=Q1373115|lt=Reiki}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.
