revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11424804]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} in Japan. His {{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=kabane}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamatonoaya clan|en|Yamatonoaya clan|WD=Q10870550|lt=Yamato no Aya clan}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|WD=Q1117901|lt=Takichi District, Yamato Province}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|en|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|WD=Q11638487|lt=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takechi|en|Prince Takechi|WD=Q837590|lt=Prince Takeda}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki (era)|en|Reiki (era)|WD=Q1373115|lt=Reiki}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779}}}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} in Japan. His {{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=kabane}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Naohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Yamato Capital|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamatonoaya clan|en|Yamatonoaya clan|WD=Q10870550|lt=Yamato no Aya clan}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|WD=Q1117901|lt=Takichi District, Yamato Province}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|en|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|WD=Q11638487|lt=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Fukei Ootomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takechi|en|Prince Takechi|WD=Q837590|lt=Prince Takeda}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki (era)|en|Reiki (era)|WD=Q1373115|lt=Reiki}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Sakanoue no Munehiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
== References == * {{ill|Kojima Noriyuki|ja|小島憲之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Noriyuki|1=Noriyuki Kojima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460159}}, Naoki Takatsukasa, Nishimiya Kazutami, Kurashima Susumu, and Mori Masamori, eds. and translated. ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Volume 3''. Shōgakukan (New Edition Japanese Classical Literature Collection 4), 1998. * {{ill|Aoki Kazuo|ja|青木和夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoki Kazuo|1=Kazuo Aoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11661670}}, Inaoka Kōji, Sasayama Haruo, and Shirafuji Hiroyuki, eds. ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Q11424779|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu|1=Emperor Bidatsu}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|WD=Q1117901|lt=Takichi District, Yamato Province|1=Takaichi District, Nara}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|en|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|WD=Q11638487|lt=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Ōtomo no Fukei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takechi|en|Prince Takechi|WD=Q837590|lt=Prince Takeda|1=Prince Takechi}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki (era)|en|Reiki (era)|WD=Q1373115|lt=Reiki|1=Reiki (era)}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Q11424779|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan|1=Daijō-kan}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>

''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, Volumes 2–3''. Iwanami Shoten (New Japanese Classical Literature Collection 13–14), 1990–1991. * Naoki Takatsukasa. "Jinshin no Ran and the Sakanoue Clan." ''{{ill|Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research on the Shoku Nihongi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', Vol. 1, Issue 1, January 1954.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:坂上熊毛]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|children={{ill|Q11424779|WD=Q11424779|1=Q11424779}}}} '''Sakanoue no Kumake''' (坂上 熊毛) was a figure of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} in Japan. His {{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=kabane|1=Kabane}} was {{ill|Naohi|ja|直 (姓)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naohi|1=Atai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526687}}. He held the Rank of {{ill|Jū Daikin lower|ja|大錦下|1=Dai Kinge|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jū Daikin lower|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439798}}, which was conferred posthumously (贈位). During the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}} in 672, Kumake served as a steward (留守司) of the {{ill|Yamato Capital|ja|倭京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Capital|1=Q11387076|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11387076}}, where he aligned himself with Prince Ōama (later {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}).
== Lineage == The {{ill|Sakanoue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} was a branch of the {{ill|Yamatonoaya clan|en|Yamatonoaya clan|WD=Q10870550|lt=Yamato no Aya clan|1=Yamatonoaya clan}}, a group of immigrants from the continent. The Sakanoue clan's stronghold was in southern {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}, and they often engaged in military affairs. Kumake's son was {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Q11424779|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}.
According to the "Sakanoue Genealogy" (坂上系図)<ref>"Sakanoue Genealogy" (contained in the {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}}, Vol. 185).</ref>, Kumake was the grandson of {{ill|Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja|東漢駒子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi no Aya no Komako|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>The exact timing of the adoption of the "Sakanoue no Ason" surname is unclear. Considering that {{ill|Sakanoue no Komaro|ja|坂上子麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Komaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used the compound surname during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|WD=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu|1=Emperor Bidatsu}}, it is presumed that the Sakanoue clan began using this surname after Emperor Bidatsu's era.</ref>, and the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja|坂上甲由|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Kōyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Given that his father was based in {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|WD=Q1117901|lt=Takichi District, Yamato Province|1=Takaichi District, Nara}}, it is likely that Kumake also originated from the same area.
== Career == During the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}, Sakanoue no Kumake served as a steward of the Yamato Capital (倭京), distinct from the primary capital located in {{ill|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|en|Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|WD=Q11638487|lt=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}}.<ref>The following details are based on the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', Vol. 28, Tenmu 1st year, 6th month, 29th day (己丑), continued entry.</ref> The term Yamato (倭) referred to the administrative center distinct from the capital near Lake Biwa. Another steward, {{ill|Takasaka no Ō|ja|高坂王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaka no Ō|1=Prince Takasaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669026}}, worked alongside or under Kumake.
In late June 672, when Prince Ōama declared rebellion, Emperor Kobun (Prince Ōtomo) dispatched envoys to the Yamato Capital to mobilize troops. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Ōtomo no Fukei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takechi|en|Prince Takechi|WD=Q837590|lt=Prince Takeda|1=Prince Takechi}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki (era)|en|Reiki (era)|WD=Q1373115|lt=Reiki|1=Reiki (era)}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Q11424779|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan|1=Daijō-kan}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>

''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, Volumes 2–3''. While Takasaka no Ō and envoys such as {{ill|Hozumi no Momomochi|ja|穂積百足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Momomochi|1=Hozumi no Momotari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597083}}, {{ill|Hozumi no Ioe|ja|穂積五百枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi no Ioe|qq=}}, and {{ill|Mononobe no Hinata|ja|物部日向|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Hinata|1=mononobe no himuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11570695}} organized forces, {{ill|Ōtomo no Fuhito|ja|大伴吹負|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Fuhito|1=Ōtomo no Fukei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432333}} conspired with Kumake to align with Ōama. Together, they sought cooperation from other clans, including the {{ill|Yamato no Aya clan|ja|倭漢直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Aya clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
On June 29, a false report, orchestrated by Kumake and his allies, claimed that {{ill|Prince Takechi|en|Prince Takechi|WD=Q837590|lt=Prince Takeda|1=Prince Takechi}} had arrived from Fushimi with a large force, causing panic among the pro-Kobun troops. Subsequently, Kumake and other officials declared allegiance to Ōama, facilitating a bloodless takeover of the Yamato Capital by Fuhito's forces.
In {{ill|Reiki (era)|en|Reiki (era)|WD=Q1373115|lt=Reiki|1=Reiki (era)}} 2 (716), Kumake’s son, {{ill|Sakanoue no Munehiro|ja|坂上宗大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakanoue no Munehiro|1=Q11424779|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424779}}, was granted farmland in recognition of Kumake's contributions during the Jinshin War.<ref>''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}'', Vol. 7, Reiki 2nd year, 4th month, 8th day (癸丑).</ref> Later, in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 1 (757), the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan|1=Daijō-kan}} reviewed Kumake’s deeds, conferred upon him the Rank of Jū Daikin Lower (大錦下), and granted his descendants six chō of {{ill|功田|ja|功田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/功田|1=kouden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398438}} (merit land) for two generations.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Vol. 20, Tenpyō Hōji 1st year, 12th month, 9th day (壬子).</ref>
